Written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun}}-style and contained within fifty volumes, the contents cover a span of thirty years from 858 to 887 corresponding to three imperial reigns: {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|WD=Q349243|lt=Seiwa|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, {{ill|Emperor Yōzei|en|Emperor Yōzei|WD=Q349280|lt=Yōzei|1=Emperor Yōzei}}, and {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|Emperor Kōkō|WD=Q440518|lt=Kōkō|1=Emperor Kōkō}}. It contains many imperial edicts and is more detailed compared to the earlier texts. Particularly famous is a description of {{ill|Ariwara no Narihira|en|Ariwara no Narihira|WD=Q451991|lt=Ariwara no Narihira|1=Ariwara no Narihira}}.<ref name="Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten">Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten (2007:155)</ref> Parts of volumes 15, 19, and 48 are incomplete.
Also described is [[:en:869 Jogan Sanriku earthquake|an earthquake in July 869]] and a tsunami that flooded the plains of northeast Japan: “The sea soon rushed into the villages and towns, overwhelming a few hundred miles of land along the coast. There was scarcely any time for escape, though there were boats and the high ground just before them. In this way about 1,000 people were killed.” These were the same plains that were submerged in the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}, according to one account. "Analysis of sediments left by the 869 tsunami led to an estimate that the earthquake had a magnitude of 8.3."<ref>Chang, Kenneth, [https://www.nytimes.com/2011/03/22/science/22predict.html?pagewanted=2&hp "Blindsided by Ferocity Unleashed by a Fault"], ''The New York Times'', web p. 2, March 21, 2011 (March 22, 2011 p. D1 NY ed.). Retrieved 2011-03-21.</ref>
==Final National History==
{{lang|ja-latn|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} is the final text in the ''Six National Histories'' series. In 936, a national history bureau (撰国史所) was established to maintain the existing national histories as well as to continue with their compilation. A new text, {{lang|ja-latn|[[Shinkokushi]]}}, was begun. However, it remained in draft form and was never completed. The declining power of the [[Ritsuryō]] institution is cited as a cause.

{{Short description|Japanese history text of 901}} {{italic title}} {{Nihongo|'''''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'''''|日本三代実録||"The True History of Three Reigns of Japan"}}, abbreviated as '''Sandai Jitsuroku''', is an officially commissioned {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|WD=Q130436|lt=Japanese history|1=History of Japan}} text. Completed in 901, it is the sixth and final text in the [[Rikkokushi|Six National Histories]] series. It covers the years 858–887.
==Background==
Following the earlier national history ''[[Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]]'' (879), {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda|1=Emperor Uda}} ordered the compilation of the years since then. It was compiled by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|en|Fujiwara no Tokihira|WD=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara no Tokihira|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, [[Ōkura no Yoshiyuki]], and [[Mimune no Masahira]]. The text was completed in 901.
==Contents==
Written in {{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|WD=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun}}-style and contained within fifty volumes, the contents cover a span of thirty years from 858 to 887 corresponding to three imperial reigns: {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|WD=Q349243|lt=Seiwa|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, {{ill|Emperor Yōzei|en|Emperor Yōzei|WD=Q349280|lt=Yōzei|1=Emperor Yōzei}}, and {{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|Emperor Kōkō|WD=Q440518|lt=Kōkō|1=Emperor Kōkō}}. It contains many imperial edicts and is more detailed compared to the earlier texts. Particularly famous is a description of {{ill|Ariwara no Narihira|en|Ariwara no Narihira|WD=Q451991|lt=Ariwara no Narihira|1=Ariwara no Narihira}}.<ref name="Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten">Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten (2007:155)</ref> Parts of volumes 15, 19, and 48 are incomplete.
Also described is [[:en:869 Jogan Sanriku earthquake|an earthquake in July 869]] and a tsunami that flooded the plains of northeast Japan: “The sea soon rushed into the villages and towns, overwhelming a few hundred miles of land along the coast. There was scarcely any time for escape, though there were boats and the high ground just before them. In this way about 1,000 people were killed.” These were the same plains that were submerged in the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}, according to one account. "Analysis of sediments left by the 869 tsunami led to an estimate that the earthquake had a magnitude of 8.3."<ref>Chang, Kenneth, [https://www.nytimes.com/2011/03/22/science/22predict.html?pagewanted=2&hp "Blindsided by Ferocity Unleashed by a Fault"], ''The New York Times'', web p. 2, March 21, 2011 (March 22, 2011 p. D1 NY ed.). Retrieved 2011-03-21.</ref>
==Final National History==
{{lang|ja-latn|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} is the final text in the ''Six National Histories'' series. In 936, a national history bureau (撰国史所) was established to maintain the existing national histories as well as to continue with their compilation. A new text, {{lang|ja-latn|[[Shinkokushi]]}}, was begun. However, it remained in draft form and was never completed. The declining power of the [[Ritsuryō]] institution is cited as a cause.
==See also==
* ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'', a categorized and chronological history text of the ''Six National Histories''; valuable for reconstructing incomplete sections of {{lang|ja-latn|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== * {{NKBJ}}
== External links == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20180519144319/http://www.j-texts.com/chuko/sandai.html Text of the ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku''] (Japanese)
